coredata.dc:description: Balancing the adsorption of OH⁻ and 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) is crucial in optimizing the competing HMF oxidation reaction and oxygen evolution reaction, especially given the polymerization tendency of HMF in alkaline solutions. Herein, we present an innovative approach for rapidly synthesizing a NiFe bimetallic metal-organic framework (MOF) induced by electron-withdrawing carbon quantum dot (EW-CQD) via electron beam irradiation within 2 ​min. EW-CQD serve as structural regulators, expanding the NiFe-MOF interlayer spacing, increasing reactive site availability, and more effectively balancing the adsorption of OH− and HMF, thereby significantly boosting the oxidation activity of HMF. The resulting EW-CQD-MOF exhibits a low potential of 1.36 ​V vs. RHE at 10 ​mA ​cm⁻2 and maintains excellent durability over 120 ​h. Comprehensive in situ characterization elucidates the HMF oxidation reaction pathway, showing high selectivity towards 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA) under ambient conditions, with an impressive HMF conversion rate of 94% and FDCA selectivity of 96% within 6 ​h. These findings underscore the critical role of structural optimization and adsorption balance in catalytic performance enhancement and offer valuable insights for designing high-efficiency catalysts, advancing sustainable catalytic processes.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Prof. Minghong Wu received the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars in 2010, was appointed as the Changjiang Scholar Distinguished Professorship by the Ministry of Education in 2012, and was elected as an academician of the Chinese Academy of Engineering in 2021. She has dedicated her career to teaching and research in the fields of multimedia environment collaborative protection and comprehensive management. Prof. Wu has published more than 300 SCI papers in academic journals, including Nature and Nature Chemistry, holds 65 national invention patents, and authored two monographs. She has been recognized as a global highly cited scientist. Her achievements have earned her two second prizes of the National Natural Science Award, three first prizes in provincial and ministerial Science and Technology awards, the Guanghua Engineering Youth Science and Technology Award from the Chinese Academy of Engineering, as well as the titles of National Model Teacher, National Women's Contribution Model and numerous other honors and awards.

The project was funded by Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2023JJ60522). This work is Supported by Shanghai Technical Service Center of Science and Engineering Computing, Shanghai University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of synthetic process of the EW-CQD-MOF; (b,c) TEM images of (b) NiFe-MOF and (c) EW-CQD-MOF; (d,e) High-resolution TEM images of (d) NiFe-MOF and (e) EW-CQD-MOF; (f,g) AFM images of (f) NiFe-MOF and (g) EW-CQD-MOF; (h) EDX mapping images of EW-CQD-MOF.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, and (c) FT-IR spectra of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF; (d–h) XPS survey (d) and high-resolution XPS of C 1s (e), O 1s (f), Ni 2p (g), and Fe 2p (h) of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF; (i) N 1s of EW-CQD-MOF.

(a) LSV curves of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF in 1.0 ​M KOH with 50 ​mM HMF and (b) corresponding Tafel plots; (c) EIS data of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF; (d) OCP curves of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF in 1.0 ​M KOH with or without addition of 50 ​mM HMF; (e) Zeta potential of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF; (f) Static contact angles of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF in the presence of HMF and KOH; (g) Electrochemical setup for the oxidation of HMF; (h) The stability test of HMFOR for EW-CQD-MOF.

(a,b) In-situ FT-IR spectra of HMF adsorption on (a) EW-CQD-MOF and (b) NiFe-MOF; (c) In-situ Raman spectra of EW-CQD-MOF at 1.60 ​V vs. RHE in 1.0 ​M KOH with 10 ​mM HMF; (d) HPLC traces of HMF oxidation catalyzed by EW-CQD-MOF; (e) Conversion changes of HMF and yield efficiency of its oxidation products during HMF electrooxidation; (f) Reaction pathway for HMFOR in alkaline solution.

(a,b) TEM images of (a) ED-CQD-MOF and (b) B-CQD-MOF; (c,d) AFM images of (c) ED-CQD-MOF and (d) B-CQD-MOF; (e) FT-IR spectra of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF; (f) High-resolution XPS Ni 2p spectra of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF; (g) LSV curves of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF in 1.0 ​M KOH with 50 ​mM HMF and (h) corresponding Tafel plots; (i) EIS data of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Balancing the adsorption of OH⁻ and 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) is crucial in optimizing the competing HMF oxidation reaction and oxygen evolution reaction, especially given the polymerization tendency of HMF in alkaline solutions. Herein, we present an innovative approach for rapidly synthesizing a NiFe bimetallic metal-organic framework (MOF) induced by electron-withdrawing carbon quantum dot (EW-CQD) via electron beam irradiation within 2 ​min. EW-CQD serve as structural regulators, expanding the NiFe-MOF interlayer spacing, increasing reactive site availability, and more effectively balancing the adsorption of OH− and HMF, thereby significantly boosting the oxidation activity of HMF. The resulting EW-CQD-MOF exhibits a low potential of 1.36 ​V vs. RHE at 10 ​mA ​cm⁻2 and maintains excellent durability over 120 ​h. Comprehensive in situ characterization elucidates the HMF oxidation reaction pathway, showing high selectivity towards 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA) under ambient conditions, with an impressive HMF conversion rate of 94% and FDCA selectivity of 96% within 6 ​h. These findings underscore the critical role of structural optimization and adsorption balance in catalytic performance enhancement and offer valuable insights for designing high-efficiency catalysts, advancing sustainable catalytic processes.

The ultra-thin NiFe-MOF nanosheets induced by carbon quantum dots have been rapidly prepared by electron beam irradiation. EW-CQD-MOF has high catalytic oxidation activity and excellent stability of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural, which can effectively balance the adsorption of HMF and OH− and highly selective oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural to 2,5-furan dicarboxylic acid.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass has surfaced as a remarkably promising renewable resource in the pursuit of carbon neutrality and maintaining sustainability. Within this domain, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) has gained prominence as a pivotal platform chemical due to its significant potential in synthesizing multiple chemicals of high added value. Among the selective oxidation products of HMF, 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA) has attracted considerable focus owing to the critical role of polyethylene 2,5-furandicarboxylate, which is a viable alternative for petroleum-derived polyethylene terephthalate [1].

Compared with aerobic oxidation, the electrochemical HMF oxidation has garnered significant interest because of its distinct advantages, including mild reaction conditions, simple use device, cleanliness, high efficiency, and environmental friendliness [2]. Precious metal-containing electrocatalysts have demonstrated low overpotential oxidation of HMF. However, they suffer from high costs and low selectivity for FDCA. Alternative materials such as metal phosphide, sulfide, and nitride have shown high FDCA yields and high faradaic efficiency under alkaline conditions. Despite their promise, these materials face significant limitations: 1) limited activity and selectivity due to rivalrous adsorption of HMF and OH−, and 2) poor long-term durability of the catalysts. Therefore, developing efficient and stable catalysts that can effectively balance the adsorption dynamics of HMF and OH− is imperative to optimize the electrochemical conversion of HMF to FDCA [3].

Metal-organic framework (MOF) have garnered considerable attention in electrocatalysis because of their versatile synthesis methods and accessible porous structures [4–6]. Two-dimensional (2D) MOF nanosheets (NSs) offer outstanding electrocatalytic properties compared to their bulk counterparts, owing to their atomic economy and increased surface area. However, conventional top-down methods for synthesizing 2D MOF NSs, including ball milling and wet chemistry, suffer from drawbacks like low yield, uncontrollable layer thickness, and lengthy synthesis times. In recent years, electron beam irradiation has become an effective approach for creating diverse nanomaterials, such as metals, oxides, semiconductors, and graphene sheets, due to its simplicity and rapid reaction times. Additionally, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), known for their plentiful active sites and cost-effectiveness, have gained wide attention in catalysis and are regarded as highly suitable for in-situ assisted production of 2D materials [7,8]. Therefore, the combined use of the electron beam irradiation technology and CQD additives offers a promising approach for the efficient synthesis of 2D MOF NSs [9].

In this study, we introduce an innovative approach for the rapid synthesis of NiFe bimetallic MOF NSs with an active interface induced by CQDs under electron beam irradiation within 2 ​min [10–12]. Using cost-effective naphthalene dicarboxylic acid ligand on a MOF composite platform, we demonstrate that CQDs play a crucial role in enhancing the catalytic performance [13–16]. The introduction of CQDs effectively increases the NiFe-MOF's layer spacing, thereby enhancing the number of reactive sites and optimizing the equilibrium adsorption of OH− and HMF. Consequently, this leads to significantly enhanced HMF oxidation activity. Moreover, we revealed the distinct effects of CQDs functionalized with electron-withdrawing/donating groups on the structural formation and HMF oxidation reaction (HMFOR) activity of NiFe-MOF NSs. Our findings indicate that CQDs functionalized with electron withdrawing groups (EW-CQD) are particularly conducive to the formation of NiFe-MOF NS structures. The EW-CQD-MOF exhibits high selectivity towards FDCA within a short reaction time under atmospheric pressure. To elucidate the HMF oxidation mechanism on EW-CQD-MOF, we employed an array of in-situ characterization techniques and continuously monitored the reaction process utilizing high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). These findings emphasize the effectiveness of electron beam irradiation as a pioneering method for the ultra-fast synthesis of high-performance MOF-based electrocatalysts. Integrating CQDs into MOF structures presents itself as a prospective strategy for adjusting the electronic and catalytic properties, thereby advancing the field of biomass conversion and sustainable chemical production.

EW-CQD-MOF was rapidly synthesized in situ by adding CQD stock solution directly via electron beam irradiation, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 a (for detailed synthesis steps, refer to the Experimental Section). This synthesis strategy not only achieves ultrafast production of 2D MOF NSs within 2 ​min but also circumvents the complex high-temperature hydrothermal treatment procedures, thus preserving the structural diversity of the materials. As depicted in Fig. S1, EW-CQD exhibits an average size about 3.02 ​nm, characterized by a distinct 3D excited emission matrix (3D-EEM) spectrum (Fig. S2) and a relatively high degree of graphitization, as confirmed by its comparatively low I D/I G ratio (Fig. S3) [17]. In X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, a broad peak centered around 2θ=20° was detected for EW-CQD (Fig. S4). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum indicates the existence of functional groups including O-H at approximately 3010 ​cm−1, C-H at 827 ​cm−1, and C=C at about 1637 ​cm−1, along with C-F (≈1052 and 1111 ​cm−1) and N-H (3207 ​cm−1) originating from its precursors O-trifluoromethylbenzoic acid and P-phenylenediamine (Fig. S5), which is further affirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements (Figs. S6a–e, Table S3). Apart from the conventional C and O elements (Figs. S6b–d), amine N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N are illustrated in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. S6c), while the high-resolution F 1s spectrum indicates the existence of C-F, C-F2, and C-F3 groups (Fig. S6e) [18].

The incorporation of EW-CQD facilitates the transformation of NiFe-MOF NSs (Fig. 1b) from thickly stacked bulk to smaller sheets with well-dispersed EW-CQD on its surface (Fig. 1c). Interplanar distances of ∼0.23 ​nm for NiFe-MOF are disclosed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1d), while lattice fringes of about 0.21 ​nm for EW-CQD confirm the successful integration of EW-CQD and NiFe-MOF NSs (Fig. 1e) [19]. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images (Fig. 1f and g) further reveal uniform thickness for NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF with an average height of about 80 ​nm and 5 ​nm, respectively [20]. The significant thickness variation indicates that CQDs effectively modulate the thickness of MOF. Comparison of samples before and after electron beam irradiation (Fig. S7) show significant structural changes and precipitation, with the synthesized sample powder exhibiting a noticeable color change to black (Fig. S8), further confirming the successful combination of EW-CQD with NiFe-MOF. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mapping of EW-CQD-MOF further certificates the existence of C, O, N, Ni, Fe, and trace amounts of F elements, suggesting the successful hybridization of EW-CQD with NiFe-MOF (Fig. 1h), with the corresponding elemental content presented in Fig. S9.

XRD was utilized to investigate the crystal structures of the samples (Fig. 2 a). The results indicated that the addition of EW-CQD does not alter the lattice structure of the original NiFe-MOF. Raman spectra further illustrated the vibration modes of the organic ligand (Fig. 2b), confirming the comprehensive coordination of metal sites with organic ligands. The Raman spectra of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF exhibit two peaks (ca. 1590 and 1386 ​cm−1), corresponding to the in-phase and out-of-phase stretching regions of the carboxylate group, respectively [21,22]. The slight blue shift of the COO− groups in complexes containing CQDs further illustrates the electron absorption effect of CQDs in these complexes.

To clarify the influence of the structure on electrocatalytic efficiency, FT-IR and XPS characterization techniques were employed to investigate the elemental valence states. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to analyze the functional groups present in EW-CQD-MOF compared to NiFe-MOF in the range of 2000–400 ​cm−1, as displayed in Fig. 2c. The adsorption bands of the asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of the carboxyl group coordinated to the metal centers are observed at 1554 and 1400 ​cm−1 respectively. Additionally, the C-H stretching region of the benzene ring divides into two components at 600 and 810 ​cm−1. The presence of ν (Ni-O) band at 480 ​cm−1 confirms the coordination between Ni atoms and the carboxyl group of 1,4-naphthalene dicarboxylic acid [23].

Apart from the initial signals of C, O, Ni, and Fe that were observed in the XPS survey spectrum of NiFe-MOF, N and F were also identified in EW-CQD-MOF (Fig. 2d), which was in accordance with the EDX spectrum. High-resolution spectra of Ni 2p, Fe 2p, C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s are presented in Fig. 2e–i and the corresponding element content are shown in Table S1. Compared to the pristine NiFe-MOF (C–O at 286.02 ​eV), the corresponding peaks in EW-CQD-MOF exhibited a slight redshift of 0.2 ​eV (C–O at 286.22 ​eV) (Fig. 2e) [24]. Since both NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF are synthesized by electron beam irradiation with the same dose, the redshift observed in the C 1s spectrum of EW-CQD-MOF can only be attributed to the introduced EW-CQD. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the C 1s spectrum of EW-CQD before irradiation shows peaks at 284.8 ​eV ​(C=C), 285.95 ​eV (C–O/C–N), 288.05 ​eV ​(C=O/C–F), and 297.73 ​eV (π–π∗ satellite), while after irradiation, the peaks are at 284.8 ​eV, 285.98 ​eV, 288.03 ​eV, and 290.7 ​eV. No evident shift can be observed in the C 1s spectrum of CQDs before and after irradiation. Therefore, the redshift in the C 1s spectrum of EW-CQD-MOF is due to the introduction of CQDs, which optimize the electronic structure of the NiFe-MOF. Additionally, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum of NiFe-MOF showed peaks located at 532.30 ​eV (C–O), 531.17 ​eV (M–O–R), and 529.39 ​eV (M ​− ​O), respectively. Upon the addition of CQDs, the corresponding peaks of EW-CQD-MOF also slightly redshifted (C–O at 532.56 ​eV, M–O–R at 531.27 ​eV, and M ​− ​O at 529.76 ​eV) (Fig. 2f–Table S2) [9]. This redshift is ascribed to the CQDs optimizing the electronic structure of the NiFe-MOF, forming a region around C and O atoms that loses electrons.

Compared with NiFe-MOF, the two peaks in the Ni 2p spectrum of EW-CQD-MOF are blue-shifted by 0.66 (Ni 2p3/2) and 0.49 ​eV (Ni 2p1/2), respectively (Fig. 2g) [25]. This phenomenon indicates a modification of the local electronic-withdrawing structure of Ni3+, resulting in a lower valence state for all Ni species and implying partial conversion of Ni3+ to Ni2+ [26,27]. Notably, compared with NiFe-MOF, the two peaks in the Fe 2p spectrum of EW-CQD-MOF blueshifts by about 0.14 ​eV (Fe 2p3/2) and 0.64 ​eV (Fe 2p1/2), respectively (Fig. 2h), suggesting that the lower valence sate of Fe species in EW-CQD-MOF [28–31]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of EW-CQD-MOF was resolved into three subpeaks, located at 398.6 ​eV (pyridinic N), 399.7 ​eV (amine N), and 400.7 ​eV (pyrrolic N) (Fig. 2i) [32–34]. Additionally, the high-resolution F 1s spectrum exhibited peaks centered at 688.1 ​eV (C–F) (Fig. S10, Table S4), originating from the introduced EW-CQD [35].

The HMF electrocatalytic oxidation capacity of the EW-CQD-MOF was examined through linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) conducted in a 1.0 ​M KOH electrolyte within a single cell configuration. As shown in Fig. S11, the electrocatalytic activity of pure Ni-MOF and Fe-MOF is significantly lower than that of NiFe-MOF. Therefore, NiFe-MOF is selected as the research object. The HMF oxidation performance of NiFe-MOF at different irradiation doses (Fig. S12) shows that the sample synthesized with 50 ​KGy exhibits the highest current density and the best electrocatalytic HMF oxidation performance under 1.5 ​V vs. RHE. As illustrated in Fig. S13, the sample with 200 ​μL of CQD stock solution exhibited the highest current density at 1.5 ​V vs. RHE, with performance gradually decreasing as the CQD addition increased. According to the LSV curves for EW-CQD-MOF at different HMF concentrations (Fig. S14), the current densities at 1.6 ​V are relatively consistent, ranging between 25 and 30 ​mA ​cm−2.

Comparative LSV analysis of the EW-CQD-MOF electrode for HMF oxidation (50 ​mM HMF) and water oxidation (absence of HMF) were conducted (Fig. S15) [36]. The applied potential was about 1.36 ​V vs. RHE for HMF oxidation to attain 10 ​mA ​cm−2, notably inferior to that required for water oxidation (1.52 ​V vs. RHE), indicating a preferential oxidation of HMF on EW-CQD-MOF. It is worth noting that the typical Ni-based water oxidation peak corresponding to the Ni2+ to Ni3+ transition was absent in the presence of HMF [37]. Under 50 ​mM HMF conditions, the onset potentials for HMFOR are determined to be 1.31 and 1.36 ​V vs. RHE for EW-CQD-MOF and NiFe-MOF, respectively. Notably, the EW-CQD-MOF electrode requires 1.36 ​V vs. RHE to achieve 10 ​mA ​cm−2, significantly lower than 1.41 ​V vs. RHE for NiFe-MOF (Fig. 3 a), suggesting a reduced overpotential for HMFOR with EW-CQD-MOF [38,39]. Moreover, EW-CQD-MOF displayed a lower Tafel slope (29.04 ​mV dec−1) compared to NiFe-MOF (39.81 ​mV dec−1), indicative of enhanced electrocatalytic reaction kinetics with the integration of EW-CQD (Fig. 3b) [40,41].

Electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was approximated by plotting the change in current density (ΔJ) at 0.98 ​V vs. RHE against diverse scan rates to determine the double-layer capacitance (C dl). The C dl value of the EW-CQD-MOF surpassed that of NiFe-MOF (Fig. S16), implying a larger ECSA [42]. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was utilized to elucidate the HMFOR reaction dynamics. EW-CQD-MOF demonstrated revealed a smaller charge transfer resistance (R ct) (18 ​Ω) compared to NiFe-MOF (31 ​Ω) (Fig. 3c), with a solution resistance (R s) of approximately 6 ​Ω, suggesting advantageous electron transport and rapid catalytic kinetics.

Additionally, EW-CQD-MOF displayed superior adsorption capacity for both HMF and OH− on the catalyst surface. Additionally, open-circuit potential (OCP) measurements were utilized to assess the change in organic adsorbent content within the inner Helmholtz layer [43,44]. As shown in Fig. 3d, when 50 ​mM HMF is introduced, the OCP of EW-CQD-MOF showed a significant decrease of 520 ​mV, in contrast to a 430 ​mV decrease observed for NiFe-MOF, denoting a mightier adsorption of HMF within the inner Helmholtz layer of EW-CQD-MOF in contrast to NiFe-MOF, further substantiating its enhanced catalytic activity for HMFOR [45]. Moreover, the EW-CQD-MOF exhibited a more negative Zeta potential value (−10.4 ​mV) than that of original NiFe-MOF (−8.4 ​mV) in a KOH solution (Fig. 3e). This suggests a higher OH− concentration in the inner Helmholtz layer, which can promote the HMF oxidation [46].

The adsorption of HMF and OH− by NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF was analyzed using static contact angle tests. Pristine NiFe-MOF exhibited a stronger affinity for OH− (14.8°) and a weaker affinity for HMF (41.9°) (Fig. 3f). This discrepancy in adsorption may lead to reduced HMFOR activity due to insufficient HMF supply for HMF conversion into FDCA. On the other hand, EW-CQD-MOF demonstrated superior HMFOR performance by effectively balancing the rivalrous adsorption of HMF (27.0°) and OH− (24.0°), thereby coordinating the formation of active species and efficient supply of reactants [3]. To evaluate the stability of EW-CQD-MOF during HMFOR, chronoamperometry was conducted at 1.6 ​V vs. RHE using a flow cell setup. As illustrated in Fig. 3g, the anode was prepared by spraying EW-CQD-MOF onto the gas diffusion layer (GDL) with a loading of ∼2 ​mg ​cm−2 for HMFOR, while Ag was sprayed onto the GDL as the cathode for the CO2RR. The 1.0 ​M KOH with 5 ​mM HMF was circulated through the anode by a peristaltic pump, while a separate 1.0 ​M KOH electrolyte was circulated within the cathode [47–49]. By supplementing HMF or refreshing the electrolyte, the concentration of HMF in the electrolyte was maintained at about 5 ​mM. As electrolysis proceeded, the current density exhibited a slight decrease due to the consumption of HMF. However, with the continuous updating of the electrolyte and replenishing the HMF, the current density remained almost constant for over 120 ​h (Fig. 3h), demonstrating the superior durability of EW-CQD-MOF. In contrast, the results show that after about 25 ​h of constant voltage test, NiFe-MOF exhibits a rapid decline in current density. In addition, we have also added Pt/C material as the cathode for stability testing. As shown in Fig. S17, after a stability test lasting up to 50 ​h, the current density does not exhibit significant attenuation, which proves the excellent stability of EW-CQD-MOF. After testing for more than 50 ​h, we carefully scraped the samples from the dried carbon paper. Given the small amount of the samples sprayed onto the carbon paper, we captured TEM images to observe the morphology. As shown in Fig. S18, EW-CQD-MOF still retains its nanosheet structure after the long-term stability test and CQD particles can be seen evenly distributed over the surface of the nanosheets. It can be observed that the EW-CQD-MOF after the stability test still contains two distinct types of lattice fringes, which demonstrates the excellent crystal phase stability of the material. The consistent morphology before and after the test demonstrates the excellent stability of EW-CQD-MOF.

To elucidate the adsorption behavior of the electrocatalyst towards organic molecules and determine the oxidation reaction path, in-situ FT-IR spectroscopy was employed and the test device was displayed in Fig. S19. The large vibration bands at ∼1227 ​cm−1 are ascribed to the C-OH vibration or the C-O-C vibration within the furan rings (Fig. 4 a). Since the C-OH was consumed during HMF oxidation, the significantly enhanced peak is largely attributed to the C-O-C vibration resulting from the more resonant structure upon oxidation. On the high-wavenumber side of this C=O stretch, two types of C=O stretches originating from the formyl groups evolve, one at 1695 ​cm−1 and the other at 1737 ​cm−1. Additionally, more pronounced growth of the COOH vibration at ∼1595 ​cm−1 and COO− vibration at ∼1350 ​cm−1 was observed for EW-CQD-MOF, reflecting the facilitated HMFCA oxidation into FFCA or FFCA salt on the EW-CQD-MOF catalyst. In contrast, the absorption peaks corresponding to the products on NiFe-MOF is not significant (Fig. 4b), suggesting that the EW-CQD creates microscopic channels that promote the local accumulation of HMFCA intermediates and expedite the HMFCA oxidation kinetics for directly converting HMF into FFCA and FDCA [50,51]. This finding provides a new rational design avenue for catalyst to modulate the intermediate adsorption, reaction, desorption environment for catalytic reactions involving multiple steps.

In-situ Raman spectroscopy was utilized to unveil the active interfacial phases of EW-CQD-MOF under 1.60 ​V vs. RHE and the test device was displayed in Fig. S20. As portrayed in Fig. 4c, the peaks at 490 and 580 ​cm−1 correspond to the bending and stretching vibrations of Ni3+–O, respectively. In the existence of HMF in the electrolyte, the formed NiOOH species spontaneously capture protons from HMF, resulting in the reduction of Ni(OH)2. This reduction process is indicated by the gradual disappearance of characteristic peaks with increasing reaction time. These findings confirm that the generated high-valence NiOOH species serve as the actual catalytic sites for HMFOR [1,42,52].

Quantitative analysis of HMF and its oxidation products over the reaction progression was detected using HPLC (Fig. S21). Continuous monitoring of the reaction process clearly indicates the reaction pathway (Fig. 4d). When using EW-CQD-MOF as the catalyst, the HMF concentration decreased while the FDCA level increased with the continues reaction time, exhibiting the accomplished transformation of HMF to FDCA. The DFF concentration is almost absent, whereas detectable changes in the concentration of HMFCA and FFCA suggest that FDCA is primarily generated following the pathway of HMFCA intermediate. After a reaction time of 6 ​h, the conversion of HMF reached 94%, and FDCA was produced with a high selectivity (up to 96 ​%) and yield of up to 90% (Fig. 4e). Upon comparing the performance and preparation time with other catalysts, EW-QCD-MOF exhibits relatively obvious advantages in synthesis and performance (Tables S5 and S6). The coexistence of aldehyde and hydroxy groups on HMF allows for two potential paths for the oxidation of HMF to FDCA (Fig. 4f) [53]. One pathway incorporates the preferential oxidation of aldehyde groups into carboxy groups, forming the intermediate HMFCA, while the other involves the hydroxy groups oxidation into aldehyde groups to form DFF, the specific reaction steps are provided in the Supporting Information [54]. The in-situ analysis and continuous liquid phase monitoring indicated that the HMF electrocatalytic oxidation on EW-CQD-MOF follows a reaction pathway with the generation of HMFCA as an intermediate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of CQD functional groups on NiFe-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: To further substantiate the efficacy of EW-CQD on NiFe-MOF for HMFOR applications, we synthesized two controlled samples, electron-donating group functionalized CQD (ED-CQD) and CQD containing only one precursor without extra functional groups (B-CQD) using a one-pot solvothermal method as described in our previous work for comparative analysis. Both ED-CQD and B-CQD exhibit a comparable lateral size distribution to EW-CQD ( Fig. S22) and preserve the intact graphene crystal structure (Figs. S23a–b). As depicted in Fig. S23a, the B-CQD's peak is at 25°, while that of ED-CQD is around 22°. FT-IR spectrum reveals the fundamental structure of CQD, including O-H, C=C, C–H and N–H groups (Fig. S23c). The XPS elemental survey spectra of ED-CQD and B-CQD (Fig. S24a) manifest three dominant peaks corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, respectively (Figs. S24b–d). Additionally, ED-CQD and B-CQD exhibit similar fluorescence characteristics to EW-CQD, as evidenced by their 3D-EEM spectra (Fig. S25) [17].

ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF were prepared consistent with the procedure for EW-CQD-MOF. Comparative photographs of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF before and after electron beam irradiation are shown in Fig. S26. The synthesized sample powders show a notable color change compared to the original NiFe-MOF (Fig. S27), indicating successful CQD integration. Subsequent property measurements were carried out to investigate the corresponding products. The average thicknesses of the synthesized ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF are about 45 and 53 ​nm, respectively (Fig. 5 a and b), consistent with the previous observation that CQDs promote the in-situ growth of thinner NiFe-MOF NSs [9]. Their sheet-like structure characteristics are further affirmed by the TEM images (Fig. 5c and d), with ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF being slightly thinner than the original NiFe-MOF NSs but thicker than EW-CQD-MOF, consistent with their AFM data. These results firmly establish that the introduction of CQD effectively widens the interlayer spacing of nanosheets and modulates their thickness, with EW-CQD exhibiting the most pronounced regulatory effect [54].

The difference in XRD patterns between B-CQD-MOF and ED-CQD-MOF is mainly assigned to the addition of CQDs, as depicted in Fig. S28, the peak at 38° of B-CQD-MOF is less obvious than that of ED-CQD-MOF, but the two samples exhibit similar overall structures. Raman analysis reveals a broad band at 1090 ​cm−1, revealing Ni-O interactions, according to typical vibrational modes attributed to the organic component in ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF (Fig. S29). FT-IR spectra further confirm the formation of metal-organic bonding (Fig. 5e), consistent with the infrared spectral results of NiFe-MOF and EW-CQD-MOF. XPS survey spectra and high-resolution C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and Fe 2p spectra of B-CQD-MOF and ED-CQD-MOF show peak positions similar to those in EW-CQD-MOF (Fig. S30). High-resolution Ni 2p spectrum also reveals a trend of peak shift from higher to lower valence states relative to the original NiFe-MOF (Fig. 5f). Specifically, the Ni 2p3/2 peaks of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF shift 0.48 ​eV towards lower binding energy compared to NiFe-MOF, while the Ni 2p1/2 peaks of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF shift 0.53 ​eV and 0.37 ​eV, respectively, toward lower binding energy, indicating that the CQD introduction refines the electronic structure of the NiFe-MOF [55].

We subsequently evaluated the two samples’ HMFOR performance. The LSV images (Fig. 5g) demonstrate that the ED-CQD-MOF electrode requires 1.44 ​V vs. RHE to achieve 10 ​mA ​cm−2, which is close to the original NiFe-MOF. In contrast, B-CQD-MOF requires 1.65 ​V vs. RHE to attain 10 ​mA ​cm−2, significantly higher than the original NiFe-MOF [36]. The overpotentials for HMFOR of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF exhibit a certain gap with EW-CQD-MOF, signifying that the addition of EW-CQD substantially boosts the electrochemical performance of HMFOR. Tafel slope analysis (Fig. 5h) shows that the Tafel slope of ED-CQD-MOF (61.72 ​mV dec−1) and B-CQD-MOF (65.04 ​mV dec−1)) are higher than EW-CQD-MOF, demonstrating more sluggish HMFOR kinetics in ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF, whereas EW-CQD-MOF demonstrates superior reaction kinetics. Besides, ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF exhibit significantly lower ECSA and intrinsic activity compared to EW-CQD-MOF (Fig. S31). The Nyquist plots (Fig. 5i) reveal that ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF exhibit much larger Rct than EW-CQD-MOF, indicating that EW-CQD-MOF possesses more favorable charge transfer kinetics, highly exposed active sites, and enhanced intrinsic activity [40]. The introduction of EW-CQD synergistically endow EW-CQD-MOF with excellent HMFOR performance. In addition, the adsorption capacities of ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF for HMF and OH− were also explored (Fig. S32). As observed from the open circuit voltage diagram, the introduction of 50 ​mM HMF leads to a 370 ​mV decrease in OCP for ED-CQD-MOF, while B-CQD-MOF shows a decrease of 260 ​mV, which is much lower than that for EW-CQD-MOF and NiFe-MOF. This indicates that ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF have weaker adsorption capacity for HMF. In addition, in KOH solution, ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF exhibit more positive zeta potentials compared to EW-CQD-MOF and NiFe-MOF, suggesting weaker adsorption capacity for OH−, which hampers HMF oxidation. Contact angle tests further reveal that ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF have relatively large contact angle for HMF compared to EW-CQD-MOF, indicating poorer adsorption of HMF. These factors collectively contribute to the inferior electrocatalytic oxidation performance of HMF by ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF.

In summary, we in-situ rapidly EW-CQD-induced synthesize ultra-thin NiFe-MOF NSs with a thickness of only 5 ​nm via electron beam irradiation within 2 ​min. The incorporation of electron-withdrawing groups in CQDs can expand the layer spacing of NiFe-MOF, effectively promoting the formation of 2D NiFe-MOF NSs and increase the electron transfer among C, O and metal elements. In addition, EW-CQD can better modulate the equilibrium adsorption of OH− and HMF, thus greatly improving the HMF oxidation activity of EW-CQD-MOF. EW-CQD-MOF exhibits a low potential of 1.36 ​V vs. RHE to reach 10 ​mA ​cm−2 and demonstrates excellent durability after 120 ​h of continuous operation. Notably, CQD functionalized with electron withdrawing groups are more conducive to the formation of NiFe-MOF NS structures and HMFOR processes. Furthermore, the EW-CQD-MOF exhibits high selectivity towards FDCA within a short reaction time under atmospheric pressure, achieving a 94% conversion rate of HMF within 6 ​h, indicating its broad application prospects as a high-performance HMF oxidation electrocatalyst. Our work not only enables the ultra-fast preparation achieved by electron beam irradiation but also emphasizes the importance of effectively tuning the NS structure and balancing competitive adsorption processes to improve catalytic performance. This offers a new avenue for the rational design and fabrication of efficient catalysts and contributes to the profound comprehension of the biomass oxidation mechanism, thereby advancing the field of sustainable chemical production and biomass conversion.

The ammonium hydroxide (25–28%), p-phenylenediamine (≥99%), o-(trifluoromethyl)benzoic acid (≥98%), ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 99.9%), 1,4-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid (H2NDC, 98 ​%), nickel acetate tetrahydrate (Ni(Ac)2·4H2O, ≥98%), N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC, 99.8%), trolamine (TEA, 99.5%) and the reactants such as 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA), 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF), 5-hydroxymethyl-2-furancarboxylic acid (HMFCA), 5-formyl-2-furancarboxylic (FFCA), 2,5-diformylfuran (DFF) were sourced from Adamas. The ethanol (≥99.7%), potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥90%), and isopropanol (≥99.7%) were purchased from Greagent. The Nafion solution (5 ​wt%) was obtained from Sigma Alpha, anion exchange membrane (AEM) was from Sustainion™ X37-50.

To synthesize EW-CQD, p-phenylenediamine (500 ​mg) and o-trifluoromethylbenzoic acid (500 ​mg) were dissolved in isopropanol (50 ​mL) to form a homogeneous solution via ultrasonic scattering. Subsequently, the solution was then moved to a 100 ​mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 200 ​°C with a rate of 8 ​°C per minute for 12 ​h. Upon cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 ​μm organic phase microporous membrane to eliminate contaminants. EW-CQD was completely dialyzed with deionized water following rotary evaporation. Subsequently, part of the EW-CQD stock solution was reserved for further synthesis, and the remaining portion was dried in an oven at 80 ​°C for subsequent characterization. ED-CQD and B-CQD synthesis steps are detailed in Support information.

In a standard process for synthesizing NiFe-MOF, the solution A was prepared by mixing 0.7 ​mmol Ni(Ac)2·4H2O (174.3 ​mg) and 0.1 ​mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (40.4 ​mg) in 35 ​mL deionized water with ultrasonic scattering for 0.5 ​h. Simultaneously, the solution B was prepared through mixing 0.3 ​mmol ​H2NDC (64.8 ​mg) in 35 ​mL DMAC with ultrasonic scattering for 0.5 ​h. Solution A was then slowly added to solution B with continuous stirring for 10 ​min. Subsequently, 1.6 ​mL of TEA was rapidly injected. The resulting mixture were transferred and sealed within a plastic bag, ready for the irradiation process. Using a GJ-2-II Dynamitron electron accelerator equipped with titanium window, the samples were subjected to varying levels of radiation. The radiation doses applied were incrementally increased to 10, 30, 50, 70, 100, 300, and 500 ​KGy. Finally, the product was attained through centrifugation, washed three times using ethanol, then dried in an oven (60 ​°C) for 12 ​h.

In a process similar to the NiFe-MOF synthesis, the only difference was that 200 ​μL (3.4 ​mg) of EW - CQD stock solution was additionally injected into solution B. The subsequent steps are the same as the NiFe-MOF synthesis steps mentioned above. The synthesis steps of the comparison samples ED-CQD-MOF and B-CQD-MOF are detailed in the supporting information.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qianjia Ni is pursuing her master degree in Chemical Engineering and Technology supervised by Prof. Liang Wang at School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University. Her research focuses on the mechanisms of nanocatalytic materials used in electrocatalytic furfural oxidation and oxygen evolution reactions.

Prof. Wang Liang received his Ph.D. degree from the School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Shanghai University in 2012, and completed his post-doctoral research at Shanghai University in 2014. From 2018 to 2019, he was a visiting scholar at Rice University, United States. In 2021, he was promoted to a professor of Shanghai University. From 2022 to 2023, he worked as a visiting research fellow at Nanyang Technological University (NTU), Singapore. His research focuses on the green and controllable preparation and application of carbon functional nanomaterials. In recent years, he has published over 100 SCI papers, including 20 ESI highly cited papers with over 9000 citations and an H-index of 52. He has been recognized as one of the top 2% of scientists in the world five times and has been awarded Shanghai Eastern Scholar.

Prof. Minghong Wu received the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars in 2010, was appointed as the Changjiang Scholar Distinguished Professorship by the Ministry of Education in 2012, and was elected as an academician of the Chinese Academy of Engineering in 2021. She has dedicated her career to teaching and research in the fields of multimedia environment collaborative protection and comprehensive management. Prof. Wu has published more than 300 SCI papers in academic journals, including Nature and Nature Chemistry, holds 65 national invention patents, and authored two monographs. She has been recognized as a global highly cited scientist. Her achievements have earned her 2 ​s prizes of the National Natural Science Award, three first prizes in provincial and ministerial Science and Technology awards, the Guanghua Engineering Youth Science and Technology Award from the Chinese Academy of Engineering, as well as the titles of National Model Teacher, National Women's Contribution Model and numerous other honors and awards.
